pick.rationale: This 4/5 matchup features two nearly identical teams analytically—Arkansas ranks 17th in KenPom and 20th in Torvik while Wisconsin sits 21st-22nd—but the Razorbacks hold decisive edges in offensive firepower and momentum. Arkansas boasts the nation's 5th-ranked adjusted offense led by SEC Player of the Year Darius Acuff Jr., who scored 30 points in their SEC tournament championship victory over Vanderbilt, while Wisconsin's injury concerns with Nolan Winter (ankle) and Jack Janicki (wrist) create uncertainty even with their expected returns. Both teams have similar defensive profiles (48th-50th nationally), but Arkansas's elite offense and championship momentum give them a narrow advantage in what projects as one of the most competitive second-round games.

result.text: Latest injury updates for Wisconsin's Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki Skip to main content Home Olympics NFL NCAAB NCAAW NBA Women's Sports NCAAF MLB WNBA Sports Betting Big Ten Sports SEC Sports Boxing Coaches Salaries Draft Hub Fantasy Football For The Win Formula 1 Golfweek High School Sports High School Sports Awards IndyCar MMA NASCAR National Signing Day NHL Soccer Sports Business Sports Pulse Sports Seriously Tennis WWE Youth Sports Outdoors Fantasy Bet With the People Clear Play Arizona Sports Indiana Sports Michigan Sports Ohio Sports Tennessee Sports Wisconsin Teams For Subscribers Wisconsin's Nolan Winter, Jack Janicki take 'big jump' in injury recoveries John Steppe Milwaukee Journal Sentinel Show Caption Hide Caption What Wisconsin showed in the Big Ten Tournament with NCAA ahead The Wisconsin men's basketball team heads into the 2026 NCAA Tournament as a No. 5 seed playing some of their best basketball this season. Wisconsin forwards Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki have made progress in their injury recoveries. Both players participated in non-contact drills during the team's March 16 practice. Winter is recovering from an ankle injury, while Janicki is healing from a wrist injury that required surgery. MADISON – Wisconsin forward Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki have both taken what head coach Greg Gard described as a “big jump” in their injury recoveries. “Both of them were in full-go mode, other than contact stuff, major contact stuff,” Gard said after the Badgers ’ March 16 practice. “So it’s the most any of them have done. They both ran full-court in some skeleton stuff we did. Both were in the drills.” Winter has been recovering from an ankle injury he suffered in UW’s March 4 win over Maryland. He has missed the last four games, but both Gard and Winter have expressed confidence in the seven-foot forward returning in time for the men’s NCAA Tournament . He has nearly averaged a double-double with 13.3 points and 8.6 rebounds per game. Janicki has been out since suffering a wrist injury in UW’s Feb. 17 loss at Ohio State. Wisconsin forwards Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki have made progress in their injury recoveries. Both players participated in non-contact drills during the team's March 16 practice. Winter is recovering from an ankle injury, while Janicki is healing from a wrist injury that required surgery. MADISON – Wisconsin forward Nolan Winter and Jack Janicki have both taken what head coach Greg Gard described as a “big jump” in their injury recoveries. “Both of them were in full-go mode, other than contact stuff, major contact stuff,” Gard said after the Badgers ’ March 16 practice. “So it’s the most any of them have done. They both ran full-court in some skeleton stuff we did. Both were in the drills.” Winter has been recovering from an ankle injury he suffered in UW’s March 4 win over Maryland. He has missed the last four games, but both Gard and Winter have expressed confidence in the seven-foot forward returning in time for the men’s NCAA Tournament . He has nearly averaged a double-double with 13.3 points and 8.6 rebounds per game. Janicki has been out since suffering a wrist injury in UW’s Feb. 17 loss at Ohio State. He had a procedure on Feb. 19 and has missed the Badgers’ last eight games. He has held a key defensive role for the 2025-26 Badgers, averaging 16.5 minutes in 26 games. The positive news from the Badgers’ March 16 practice comes three days before they begin their NCAA Tournament run against 12th-seeded High Point on March 19 in Portland. (UW would then play either fourth-seeded Arkansas or 13th-seeded Hawaii.) “Everybody felt pretty good coming out of it, so the plan is to take another step tomorrow,” Gard said.
